records.title: Your FiveM development roadmap.
records.snippet: One curriculum, three paths: Lua development, server development, and the FiveM API — natives, entities, peds, and vehicles.

records.title: Your first line of Lua
records.snippet: Every coder starts the same way: one line, one message, one visible result. In this lesson that result is Hello, Los Santos in your FiveM server console. By the end you will understand a string, a variable, and the print

records.title: Install the FiveM client
records.snippet: Before you can write a single line of Lua you need a working FiveM. Ten minutes here removes 90% of the "nothing works" feeling later. Just a download, an install, and one Cfx.re sign-in.

records.title: Variables, numbers, strings
records.snippet: Last lesson you stored one piece of text in one variable. Now you will store several values, like a server name, slot count, and version number, then join them into one clean console line. This is your first real practic

records.title: FiveM natives: the toolbox
records.snippet: You already know some Lua. You already know there is a client side and a server side. Now you need the toolbox. FiveM gives you lots of built-in functions called natives. They let you do real game things like move a ped,

records.title: Who's in charge? Trust and authority
records.snippet: This is one of the most important lessons in the whole course. If this rule clicks, many FiveM security ideas stop feeling complicated. If it does not click, even a small script can become easy to exploit. The rule is sh

records.title: TriggerEvent: how scripts talk to each other
records.snippet: A real FiveM server runs lots of resources at the same time. They do not share variables directly, so they need a way to talk. That is what events are. An event is just a named message. One script fires it, another scrip

records.title: Crossing the wall: TriggerServerEvent and TriggerClientEvent
records.snippet: Last lesson was about events on one side. This lesson is about sending a message across the wall. The client asks. The server checks. The server answers or reacts. That small pattern sits underneath many real FiveM featu

records.title: Callbacks: ask a question, get an answer
records.snippet: Events are good when you just want to send a message and move on. But some situations need a reply. For example: "do I have enough money to buy this?" In that case the client asks a question, waits, and gets an answer ba

records.title: ox_lib: the toolbox every modern FiveM script uses
records.snippet: So far you have built things the long way so you could see how they work. Now it is time to use the standard toolbox real FiveM servers rely on. ox_lib gives you ready-made pop-ups, menus, dialogs, and progress bars so y

records.title: Frameworks: ESX, QBCore, Qbox + ox_lib
records.snippet: Real FiveM servers usually run a framework. A framework answers the same boring questions for every script: who is this player, how much money do they have, what job are they on, what inventory do they carry. This lesson

records.title: ox_target: "press E to interact", done right
records.snippet: Most FiveM servers have things players can interact with: NPCs, doors, trunks, registers, ATMs. Before ox_target, building those interactions meant lots of repeated distance checks and prompt code. With ox_target, most o

records.title: /heal command, your first script
records.snippet: By the end of this lesson you will type /heal in chat and watch your character refill to full health, on a real server, with cheating already blocked.

records.title: Build a teleport menu, step by step
records.snippet: By the end of this lesson you will type /tpmenu, click a place name, and watch your character arrive there with a smooth fade.

records.title: If / else / elseif: how code makes decisions
records.snippet: So far your scripts have run from top to bottom and always done the same thing. This lesson changes that. You will teach Lua to choose: look at a value, ask a question about it, and take a different path based on the ans

records.title: Capstone: a mini bank, step by step
records.snippet: By the end of this lesson you will run a working /bank command that lets a player deposit cash, withdraw cash, and check a balance saved in MySQL.

records.title: SQL from zero: oxmysql
records.snippet: Every script you have built so far forgets everything on restart. Cooldowns, bank balances, notes -- all gone. A database is the fix. It is a program that stores rows of data on disk and gives them back when you ask. Thi

records.title: Server-only banking with audit logs
records.snippet: In Lesson 21 you built a mini bank that held balances in memory. Restart the server and all the money vanished. Now you fix that. This lesson reworks the banking script to persist balances in SQL, adds an audit log that

records.title: Spawning entities safely
records.snippet: Everything you see in the game world -- every car, every pedestrian, every traffic cone -- is an entity. In this lesson you learn how to create them from code, track them in a table, and delete them when you are done. Th

records.title: Cleanup discipline
records.snippet: Spawning is easy. Cleaning up is the part that separates scripts that work from scripts that destroy servers. If you spawn entities and never delete them, the world fills up with ghost objects, entity counts climb, and e

records.title: Vehicles: spawn, modify, store
records.snippet: Vehicles are the most common entity you will work with in FiveM. Garage scripts, dealerships, police cruisers, test commands -- they all start with spawning a car and putting the player in it. This lesson covers the full

records.title: Handling basics and safe edits
records.snippet: Every vehicle in FiveM has a hidden file that controls how it drives: how fast it accelerates, how hard it brakes, how much grip the tires have. That file is called handling.meta. In this lesson you will learn what it is

records.title: Debugging like a developer
records.snippet: Something will break. That is not a maybe, it is a certainty. The difference between a beginner and a developer is not that the developer writes perfect code. The difference is that the developer knows how to read the er

records.title: Loops: for, while, and when to stop
records.snippet: If you copy-paste the same line over and over, you probably need a loop. A loop runs a block of code again and again until you stop it. In FiveM, knowing when to stop matters a lot, because a bad loop can hang a server.

records.title: Functions: naming a piece of code
records.snippet: You already use functions even if you have not written many yet. print is a function. Someone gave it a name, and now you call it whenever you need it. In this lesson you will do the same thing yourself: take a piece of

records.title: Tables: the one data structure to rule them all
records.snippet: In Lua, almost every collection of data is a table. Player lists, config files, inventories, job data, and many other things all use tables. This lesson teaches you how to read them, write them, loop through them, and av

records.title: Anatomy of a resource
records.snippet: A FiveM resource is just a folder with a label card. That label card is fxmanifest.lua. It tells FiveM what this folder is, which files belong to it, and which side should run them. This lesson teaches you to read that c

records.title: Client, server, shared: three folders, one resource
records.snippet: Open almost any serious FiveM resource and you will see the same three folders: client/, server/, and shared/. Think of them as three rooms with three different jobs. This lesson teaches what belongs in each room so your

records.title: Start, restart, stop: your daily dev loop
records.snippet: FiveM development is mostly one small loop: edit, save, ensure, read the console, test. Beginners often think they need more tricks than that. They do not. This lesson is about making that loop feel normal and easy.

records.title: Two worlds, one resource
records.snippet: This is one of the first big FiveM ideas that confuses beginners. Your resource folder looks like one thing, but it actually runs as two separate programs: one on the server and one on each player's PC. They do not share

records.title: Your first command, one line at a time
records.snippet: Never coded before? Perfect. Today you'll type two tiny scripts and watch them work. You don't need a server, an editor, or any installs. Your browser runs everything. By the end, you'll have made a chat command and a ti

records.title: Variables, conditionals, tables
records.snippet: Yesterday you made code run. Today you make code remember things, choose between options, and group related values together. This is still beginner territory. No fancy math. No hidden tricks. Just three building blocks y

records.title: Events and net messaging
records.snippet: This is the day where FiveM stops being "just Lua" and becomes multiplayer scripting. One side talks. The other side answers. That is all an event is. If you are starting from zero, keep one sentence in your head the who

records.title: ox_lib: the modern UX toolkit
records.snippet: ox_lib is a ready-made toolbox for player-facing UI. Instead of building every pop-up and menu from scratch, you fill in a small table and let the library do the heavy lifting. Today you start with the two easiest wins:

records.title: ESX fundamentals
records.snippet: ESX (es_extended) is one of the big FiveM frameworks. Framework code can look heavy at first, but today's idea is still small: get the player, check the cash, move money between two buckets. That is all your /deposit com

records.title: QBCore fundamentals
records.snippet: QBCore (qb-core) is the other big framework. The names are different from ESX, but the idea is the same: get the player, check the cash, move money safely. Today you write the same /deposit command again, this time in QB

records.title: Qbox and one script for all three
records.snippet: This is the hardest day in the starter kit, so slow down. You are not learning a brand-new money idea now. You are learning how one script can survive in three framework worlds. Keep repeating the same sentence: same scr

records.title: Starter-kit final tests
records.snippet: Green lab tests prove you can follow a template. These final tests prove you can rebuild the ideas yourself. Start from a blank file for each one. Tests 1 to 3 can still be treated as website thought-exercises if you wan

records.title: Your first real FiveM scripts, in your browser.
records.snippet: Seven days. One lesson per day. Read the idea, do the lab, fix the failing line, and try again. No server setup. No messy installs. Just you, the editor, and clear feedback.

records.title: What should I build first?
records.snippet: Start with a script small enough to finish today. Do not begin with housing, gangs, phones, or a full economy.

records.title: FiveM and Lua error catalog
records.snippet: Search common console errors. Each entry says what the message means, the cause that hits 90 percent of the time, and the smallest fix to try first. Use the filter chips to narrow by language or framework.

records.title: Framework matrix: QBCore vs ESX vs ox_lib
records.snippet: Same task, three frameworks. Pick the one your server uses, then read the others to understand tutorials, snippets, and Discord answers written for a different stack. Examples are deliberately short. Real code adds check

records.title: Beginner resources for Lua, FiveM, QBCore, and ESX
records.snippet: English stays the main language of the course. If you want Spanish, French, or German, use the language control in the navbar and the page will switch with the rest of the course. This page keeps the tools in one place s

records.title: How to install a FiveM script
records.snippet: A script is not just one file. It is a resource folder with a manifest, code, configs, assets, SQL, and dependencies. Install it like a lab: read, inspect, place, ensure, test, document.

records.title: Plan a FiveM script before you write any code
records.snippet: You have an idea for a script and you do not know where to start. This page is the cheatsheet. Read it once, pin the checklist at the end, and follow the same path every time you build something new. Plain English, no ja

records.title: Debug capture
records.snippet: One log helper, one capture wrapper around pcall. Errors print with traceback. The shape every safe handler reuses.

records.title: Entity registry
records.snippet: Every spawned entity gets an id and a cleanup path. On onResourceStop the registry walks itself and deletes anything still alive. Zero leaks at restart.

records.title: Event gateway
records.snippet: Client asks. Server checks every field. Server answers with a result event. The shape every safe net interaction reuses.

records.title: Script templates hub
records.snippet: Use these as starter code, not final systems. Each template includes purpose, dependencies, warning, and next steps.

records.title: Map asset QA
records.snippet: Use this before adding maps, MLOs, interiors, ymap files, or large asset packs to production.

records.title: ox_target
records.snippet: Add a global vehicle target on start, remove it on stop. Target selection is the prompt only. Server still validates the action.

records.title: Prop interaction
records.snippet: Use this when a script needs one world object, one interaction, and one cleanup path.

records.title: Resource skeleton
records.snippet: The minimum FiveM resource: a manifest, a shared logger, and the client / server split. Start every new resource from this shape.

records.title: SQL save and load
records.snippet: One save, one load. Always with ? placeholders and a params array. Citizenid as the key. The shape every persistence query reuses.

records.title: Vehicle spawn
records.snippet: Client asks. Server checks job, money, and cooldown. Server spawns and networks the vehicle. Client takes ownership and cleans up on resource stop.

records.title: Structured logging discipline
records.snippet: You will create one logger helper used by every script file.

records.title: Entity ownership and cleanup
records.snippet: You will track spawned entities and clean them on resource stop.

records.title: QBCore and ESX practical patterns
records.snippet: You will read player data on the server and avoid client authority.

records.title: Expansion modules for production FiveM
records.snippet: After the 28-lesson core, these modules pick up where the curriculum ends: thread budgets, native families, framework patterns, SQL discipline, persistence, and rollback.

records.title: Native families in production
records.snippet: You will group natives by entity, ped, vehicle, and HUD use.

records.title: Natives reading checklist
records.snippet: You will read native docs without guessing the side, handle, or expected return.

records.title: Performance and reliability basics
records.snippet: You will replace a tight loop with a throttled loop.

records.title: Prop and ped systems
records.snippet: You will store placed object state without trusting client labels.

records.title: Spec, logging, and rollback plan
records.snippet: You will write a small feature spec before code changes.

records.title: SQL failures and slow query logs
records.snippet: You will catch SQL failures and log query context.

records.title: SQL migrations and schema discipline
records.snippet: You will run one versioned migration and verify the schema row.

records.title: Vehicle persistence base
records.snippet: You will save a vehicle plate with validated server data.

records.title: Concept and scope
records.snippet: Write the server before buying the server.

records.title: Goodleaf Hosting full setup
records.snippet: Watch a complete server-owner walkthrough: Goodleaf VPS, server files, MariaDB, connection test, and your first Quasar script.

records.title: Server path · run a FiveM server
records.snippet: One path for owners who do not write code yet. Learn to run the box: tools, artifacts, MariaDB, framework, txAdmin, staff, and QA.

records.title: QA and launch
records.snippet: Public launch is a gate, not a guess.

records.title: Resources and assets
records.snippet: Know what you install before production sees it.

records.title: Stack and dependencies
records.snippet: Choose QBCore or ESX before buying scripts, then map what each script needs before it can start.

records.title: Staff and permissions
records.snippet: Separate roleplay trust from admin power.

records.title: Tools and latest artifacts
records.snippet: Install the owner toolkit before scripts, frameworks, or paid resources.

records.title: txAdmin and server.cfg
records.snippet: Learn the control room and the boot checklist. The goal is simple: start each script after the scripts it depends on.

records.title: Move from the website to a real FiveM dev setup
records.snippet: This page is optional. The website itself should teach the ideas from zero. Use this page later, when you want to take the scripts you learned here and practice them on a real local FiveM server.

records.title: Entity cleanup: spawn, register, delete
records.snippet: Every entity you spawn must be deleted. Every blip you add must be removed. Without an onResourceStop sweep, restarts stack ghosts on the map until the server reboots.

records.title: Events: trigger, listen, secure
records.snippet: Four verbs for FiveM events. Pick one based on which side fires and which side listens. The secure wrapper goes on top once the basic flow works.

records.title: One-page references, pinned beside the lesson.
records.snippet: Cheatsheets · Starter references

records.title: Lua syntax: variables, strings, operators, scope
records.snippet: The smallest Lua reference you need before writing FiveM scripts. Six rules that explain ninety percent of the syntax errors beginners hit.

records.title: ox_lib UI: notify, dialog, context, input, progress
records.snippet: The five UI primitives every modern FiveM script reaches for. All client-side, all from ox_lib. Copy-safe shapes you can drop into any resource.

records.title: ox_target: zones, entities, models, bones
records.snippet: Four ways to attach an interaction in FiveM. Pick by what you are targeting. Always remove what you add when the resource stops.

records.title: oxmysql: parameterized queries without injection
records.snippet: Five queries you reuse in every FiveM resource that touches the database. Always with ? placeholders. Never with string concatenation.

records.title: Resource skeleton: fxmanifest, client, server
records.snippet: The smallest FiveM resource that runs. Five fields in fxmanifest.lua, three script folders, and one rule about which side gets which file.

records.title: Tables: lists, maps, and pairs
records.snippet: Lua has one collection type. The same table can act as a list, a map, or a struct. The bug most beginners hit is mixing the two and breaking the iterator.
